card.intuition: A hash table maps keys to array indices via a hash function. Collisions (two keys mapping to the same index) are resolved by chaining (linked lists) or open addressing (probing). Load factor α = n/m controls resize: rehash when α > threshold.
card.analogy: A cloakroom that assigns tickets by the first letter of your name. Many people share the same first letter (collision), so each slot holds a list of coats (chaining) or the attendant checks nearby hooks (open addressing).
card.time_complexity: O(1) average for insert/search/delete; O(n) worst case with bad hash or many collisions
card.space_complexity: O(n + m) where m = table size
when_to_use: Fast key-value lookup with O(1) average access
gotchas: A good hash function distributes keys uniformly — poor distribution degrades to O(n)
Load factor threshold: typically resize at α > 0.75 (Java HashMap) — rehashing is O(n) amortised
Python dict uses open addressing with pseudo-random probing; Java HashMap uses chaining

exercise.prompt: Implement a simple hash table with chaining (separate chaining collision resolution).
hints: Iterate over self.buckets[idx] — the chain (list of (key, value) pairs) at the hashed index
exercise.test_code:
ht = HashTable()
ht.put('a', 1); ht.put('b', 2); ht.put('c', 3)
assert ht.get('a') == 1
assert ht.get('b') == 2
ht.put('a', 99)           # update
assert ht.get('a') == 99
# Force resize by inserting many keys
for i in range(20): ht.put(i, i*2)
for i in range(20): assert ht.get(i) == i*2
try:
    ht.get('missing')
    assert False, 'Should raise'
except KeyError:
    pass
print('PASS')
